1.exposition: Here is the storm-center of the whole book, and of Christian eschatology. An angel descends with the key of the abyss and a great chain, seizes the dragon, that ancient serpent, who is the devil and Satan, binds him a thousand years, and shuts and seals him in the pit “that he should deceive the nations no more” (v.3). Then John sees thrones, and the souls of those beheaded for their witness, who lived and reigned with Christ a thousand years — “the first resurrection.” The section closes on the fifth beatitude: blessed and holy is the one who shares in it, over whom the second death has no power. No passage has divided faithful readers more; yet beneath the debate runs one steady pastoral note — the martyrs reign. The very ones the beast killed are the ones now enthroned, and the dragon who seemed to triumph is on a chain.
1.original_language: Everything turns on a few words. Satan is bound (Greek edēsen, “he tied fast”) and shut in the abyssos, the same deep from which the beast had risen. The reign lasts chilia etē, “a thousand years” — in Revelation’s symbolic arithmetic a thousand is ten cubed, the sign of a full and complete span, which some weigh against the passage’s plain narrative order. The hinge of the whole debate is the verb ezēsan in v.4, “they came to life”: read as bodily resurrection it yields a literal millennium; read as the believer’s entry into life at death or conversion it yields a spiritual one. The same verb, read two ways, divides the schools. And “the first resurrection” (heē anastasis heē prōteē) is deliberately set against “the second death” (v.6), a pairing the chapter completes at v.14.
1.historical_context: This is the seedbed of the three great millennial positions, and even-handedness asks that each be named. Premillennialism reads a literal thousand-year reign of Christ on earth after his return; it is the most ancient reading, held by latin-fathers Victorinus of Pettau and much of the ante-Nicene church (the “chiliasts”), and among later voices by reformed Gill. Amillennialism, articulated by Augustine and carried by latin-fathers Bede, reads the thousand years as the present age between Christ’s comings: Christ and the departed saints reign now, and Satan is restrained from deceiving the nations as he once did; grammatical-historical Ellicott notes this reading. Postmillennialism expects a coming age of gospel triumph before Christ returns (classically Jonathan Edwards and B.B. Warfield); among our witnesses grammatical-historical Barnes reads the reign as the figurative revival of the martyrs’ cause, true religion becoming the reigning spirit on earth. The enthroned saints echo Daniel 7:9, 22, where judgment is given to the saints of the Most High; the raising of the dead recalls Ezekiel 37, and Second-Temple texts such as 4 Ezra and 2 Baruch had already imagined a temporary messianic kingdom before the final age. The question is important but, as the ancient creeds show, not first-order — they confess only that Christ “will come again to judge the living and the dead.”
1.christ: The saints reigned with Christ: their rule is wholly derivative, a share in the dominion the risen Lamb already holds. The “first resurrection” is grounded in his own, for he is the firstborn from the dead, and union with him guarantees that those who died in him live. The binding of the dragon executes the victory won at the cross, where the strong man was bound (Matthew 12:29) and the ruler of this world cast out (John 12:31). However one dates the thousand years, the reign in view is Christ’s reign, shared with his own.

verses.note: An angel comes down from heaven with the key of the bottomless pit and a great chain. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson see him commissioned to bind and imprison the dragon; the key is authority over the abyss — the very deep from which the locusts and the beast had risen (9:1; 11:7), now the prison into which Satan is cast. latin-fathers Victorinus and Bede read the great chain as the figure of the mighty restraint about to be laid on the devil’s power.

The angel laid hold on the dragon, named fourfold (as at 12:9) — the old serpent, the Devil, Satan — and bound him a thousand years. Here the crux opens. latin-fathers Bede, after Augustine, reads the binding as the restraint of Satan’s power through the present gospel age; latin-fathers Victorinus and reformed Gill, as a future literal imprisonment during a literal millennium. Either way the strong man is bound (Matthew 12:29): the adversary who ranged at will is now held on a chain.

He is cast into the abyss, shut up, and sealed — the threefold act (grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson) marking a secure, complete confinement — “that he should deceive the nations no more, till the thousand years should be fulfilled.” In the amillennial reading this is the curbing of his deceiving power now; in the premillennial, his removal during the millennium. latin-fathers Victorinus and Bede mark the sting in the tail: “after that he must be loosed a little season” (vv.7–9) — the binding is real but not yet final.

The heart of the chapter: John sees thrones, and judgment given to those who sat on them, and the souls of the beheaded who had refused the beast, his image, and his mark (ch. 13) — “they lived and reigned with Christ a thousand years.” reformed Gill and latin-fathers Victorinus read a literal bodily resurrection and earthly reign; latin-fathers Bede, the martyrs’ souls reigning with Christ in heaven now, the resurrection spiritual; grammatical-historical Barnes, the figurative revival of their cause. puritan Henry fixes on who they are: the faithful who would not bow to the beast are precisely the ones enthroned.

“The rest of the dead lived not again until the thousand years were finished. This is the first resurrection.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the deliberate distinction between the reigning saints and the rest. On the premillennial reading (Gill) this means two bodily resurrections — the saints first, the wicked at the end (vv.12–13); on the amillennial (Bede; grammatical-historical Ellicott), the first resurrection is spiritual while “the rest” rise bodily at the last day. The single verb ezēsan, “lived,” carries the whole weight of the debate.

The fifth of Revelation’s seven beatitudes: “Blessed and holy is he that hath part in the first resurrection.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the blessing on its partakers; over them “the second death hath no power” (v.14; 21:8), so grammatical-historical Ellicott hears their security from eternal death. They are “priests of God and of Christ” (1:6; 5:10) — the church’s royal-priestly calling fulfilled, however the reign itself is construed.

witnesses.point: The earliest commentator on Revelation reads a literal thousand-year reign — the ancient chiliast hope of the ante-Nicene church, Christ and the risen saints reigning after the dragon is bound.

Following Augustine, reads the thousand years as the present gospel age: Satan restrained from deceiving the nations, the departed saints reigning with Christ in heaven, and the first resurrection spiritual.
Takes 'they lived and reigned' figuratively — not a bodily rising but the revival of the martyrs' cause, true religion becoming the reigning, pervading spirit on earth.
Reads a literal first resurrection and earthly reign of the risen saints with Christ for a literal thousand years, distinct from the general resurrection of the rest of the dead.

external.name: Richard Bauckham
external.point: Whatever one's millennial scheme, the vision's thrust is the vindication and reign of the martyrs: those Rome killed are the ones enthroned, so the passage was written to comfort sufferers rather than to supply a chronological timetable.

1.application: Whatever your millennial map, the text was written for a church under the sword, and its comfort is fixed: the martyrs reign. Those whom Rome beheaded are not erased but enthroned, and the second death has no claim on them. Evil, however loud, operates only on a leash held by God — which is a summons to endurance, not to date-setting.

7.exposition: When the thousand years end, Satan is loosed from his prison and goes out to deceive the nations, Gog and Magog, gathering them for battle like the sand of the sea. They spread over the earth and surround the camp of the saints and the beloved city — but fire comes down from heaven and devours them. Then the devil is thrown into the lake of fire and sulfur, where the beast and the false prophet already are, to be tormented forever. The rhetoric is deliberate anticlimax: a last deception, a vast muster, and no battle at all — only an instant consuming. The satanic counter-trinity, introduced in reverse (beast and prophet in ch. 19, dragon last), is now complete in the lake, and the long rebellion is over.
7.original_language: Gōg kai Magōg are lifted from Ezekiel 38–39, where Gog of the land of Magog leads a coalition of distant nations against restored Israel. The verb for Satan’s work is planāo, “to lead astray, deceive” — the same deceiving the binding had stopped (v.3), now resumed on a world scale. “The camp of the saints” uses parembolē, the word for Israel’s wilderness encampment, merged with “the beloved city” so that the church is pictured as both pilgrim host and holy Jerusalem under final siege.
7.historical_context: In Ezekiel 38–39 Gog is destroyed by fire from heaven; in later Jewish tradition the names Gog and Magog became shorthand for the last eschatological enemy. John universalizes them to “the nations at the four corners of the earth” — no local war but the last global revolt. The fire that consumes the attackers echoes the fate of Gog (Ezekiel 38:22), the ruin of Sodom (Genesis 19:24), and the fire that vindicated Elijah (2 Kings 1:10). Interpreters divide over whether this assault is the same as Armageddon (16:16) and the war of 19:19 seen again — so the amillennial and recapitulation readings — or a distinct, post-millennial conflict, as premillennialists hold; the millennial positions diverge here once more.
7.christ: The fire needs no army: the victory belongs to God and his Christ, who “will kill with the breath of his mouth” (2 Thessalonians 2:8). The serpent consigned to the lake is the one whose head the seed of the woman was promised to bruise (Genesis 3:15); his end is the protoevangelium fulfilled. The Lamb who has already conquered (17:14) sees every hostile power gathered against his people undone in a single moment.

verses.note: “When the thousand years are expired, Satan shall be loosed out of his prison.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note that the binding was for the period only; the loosing precedes the final assault and his doom. puritan Henry reads the “little season” as the brief, final outbreak of evil — a release that tests and reveals the rebellion still latent in human hearts even after long restraint.

Loosed, Satan goes out to deceive the nations in the four quarters of the earth, Gog and Magog, gathering a host “as the sand of the sea.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott take Gog and Magog (from Ezekiel 38–39) as the symbolic name for the final, comprehensive muster of God’s enemies — whether literal future nations or the gathered powers of evil, an innumerable army raised for the last war against the saints.

They go up over the breadth of the earth and compass the camp of the saints and the beloved city — the church, God’s besieged people — “and fire came down from God out of heaven, and devoured them.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson stress the point: the final assault is crushed not by the saints’ arms but by God’s direct judgment, as at Sodom and against Gog (2 Kings 1:10; Ezekiel 38:22). There is no battle — only a gathering and a devouring.

The devil is cast into the lake of fire and brimstone, where the beast and the false prophet are (19:20), to be “tormented day and night for ever and ever.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the deceiver’s final destruction; reformed Gill, the shared and everlasting doom of the unholy trinity. Interpreters differ on the nature of final punishment, but the text’s plain weight is a decisive, unending defeat of evil and its author.

witnesses.point: The loosed Satan resumes the worldwide deception the binding had stopped, but the gathered assault is crushed by God's direct fire from heaven, not by the saints' own arms.
'Gog and Magog,' drawn from Ezekiel, names the final and comprehensive host of God's enemies gathered for the last, futile war against the beloved city.
Satan is loosed for a little season to make his last deception — the brief final outbreak of evil that ripens the world for judgment before his everlasting doom.

external.name: Grant R. Osborne
external.point: The scene is studied anticlimax: there is no real battle, only a vast gathering and an instant consuming — the final muster of evil is exposed as sheer futility before God.

7.application: The brief loosing tells the truth about evil: restraint is not yet renewal, and released deception finds a ready audience even after a thousand years. Yet no siege of the beloved city succeeds. The church’s last enemy is already sentenced, and the fire that ends him falls from heaven, not from her hand.

11.exposition: “Then I saw a great white throne and him who was seated on it,” and from his presence earth and sky flee away, finding no place. The dead, great and small, stand before the throne; the books are opened, and the book of life, and each is judged by his works. The sea, Death, and Hades give up their dead; then Death and Hades themselves are thrown into the lake of fire — the second death — and whoever is not found written in the book of life is cast in after them. This is the universal last judgment toward which the whole book has moved: the throne first seen in worship (ch. 4) now renders the verdict, in a terse, forensic style stripped of spectacle for the sake of solemnity.
11.original_language: The scene leans on Daniel: “the books were opened” recalls Daniel 7:10. Two kinds of record stand open — ta biblia, the books of deeds, and to biblion tēs zōēs, “the book of life,” the register of the redeemed. Judgment is kata ta erga, “according to their works.” Hadēs here is the realm of the dead, not the lake of fire; it is itself thrown in. And “the second death” (v.14) completes the pairing begun at v.6: the first resurrection exempts from the second death.
11.historical_context: The vision gathers the prophetic tradition of God’s judgment throne (Daniel 7:9–10) and the dual-book imagery of Scripture: books recording deeds (Malachi 3:16) and the book of life (Exodus 32:32; Daniel 12:1; Philippians 4:3). That earth and sky “fled away” looks ahead to the new heaven and new earth of the next chapter and recalls Isaiah 51:6. Judgment “according to works” alongside reliance on the book of life raises the classic question of works and grace: the mainstream reading holds that deeds are the public evidence brought into court (Romans 2:6; 2 Corinthians 5:10), while final destiny turns on whether one’s name is in the Lamb’s book — works testify, grace saves.
11.christ: The “book of life” is, throughout Revelation, the Lamb’s book (13:8; 21:27): enrollment is by union with the slain and risen Christ. The Father “has given all judgment to the Son” (John 5:22), so the one on the white throne is the very Lamb who was slain — the Judge is the Redeemer. That Death and Hades are cast into the lake fulfills his word that he holds the keys of Death and Hades, and abolishes death as the last enemy (1 Corinthians 15:26). For those written in the book the throne holds no terror; for those outside, it is the final reckoning.

verses.note: “I saw a great white throne, and him that sat on it, from whose face the earth and the heaven fled away.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read the white as the spotless purity and justice of the judgment; the seated One is God, or Christ as judge. puritan Henry marks the awful majesty: before the face of the Judge the present heaven and earth flee and vanish, finding no place (cf. 21:1; 2 Peter 3:10) — the cosmos itself cannot stand before him.

“I saw the dead, small and great, stand before God; and the books were opened… and another book… which is the book of life.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott distinguish the two: the books that record men’s deeds, and the book of life that registers the redeemed. eastern Cyril presses the standard — “judged… according to their works” (Romans 2:6; Matthew 16:27) — each weighed by what he has done, the books bearing witness, and none exempt.

“The sea gave up the dead… and death and hell delivered up the dead which were in them.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson, with reformed Gill, note the comprehensiveness: every realm that held the dead — the sea, the grave, the abode of departed spirits — surrenders them, so that the resurrection to judgment is truly universal and none is missing from the assize.

“And death and hell were cast into the lake of fire. This is the second death.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson hear the last enemy abolished (1 Corinthians 15:26): death and Hades, having yielded their dead, are themselves destroyed, so that for the redeemed there is no more death (21:4). The lake of fire is now defined — “the second death” — the dark answer to the first resurrection’s promise in v.6.

“Whosoever was not found written in the book of life was cast into the lake of fire.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Gill make the book of life the decisive register (13:8; 21:27): the one question that finally decides eternity is whether a name is written there. eastern Cyril reads the solemn close — the eternal perdition of all who are not Christ’s, the second death — the last word before the new heaven and new earth (ch. 21).

witnesses.point: The great white throne is the final, universal assize: all the dead, small and great, are judged by the deeds recorded in the opened books.
The throne is great in majesty and white in spotless justice — one before which the present heaven and earth themselves flee away and find no place.
Each is judged according to his works, the books bearing witness; yet it is the book of life that finally decides who belongs to Christ.
Death and Hades, having given up their dead, are themselves cast into the lake of fire — the abolition of death, and the second death for all not found in the book of life.

external.name: Craig R. Koester
external.point: Sets the opened books and the book of life against the ancient judicial and civic tradition — records of deeds brought into court, and a register of citizens — so that deeds are the public evidence while enrollment in the Lamb's book decides belonging.

11.application: The Great White Throne presses the gospel’s urgency and its comfort at once. Every person, great and small, will give account (cf. Hebrews 9:27); deeds are weighed, and nothing is hidden. Yet the one question that finally decides eternity is whether a name is written in the book of life. It warns against presumption and against despair alike — and the destruction of Death itself means the believer’s last enemy is already abolished.

_meta.title: The Thousand Years and the Last Judgment
reflection: Faithful Christians have read the thousand years (vv.2–6) as a future earthly reign, a present heavenly one, or a symbol of the whole church age. Can you state the view you find least persuasive in a way its holders would recognize as fair?
The very ones the beast beheaded are the ones John sees enthroned and reigning with Christ (v.4). How does “the martyrs reign” reframe a faithfulness that, from the outside, looks like defeat?

At the great white throne the deeds are weighed, but the decisive question is whether a name is written in the book of life (v.12; v.15). How do you hold together the sobriety of judgment by works and the assurance of grace recorded in the Lamb’s book?
